A life, so far

Sisir
Talluri

01 — Origins

Where it started

Chicago · July 9, 2007

I was born in Chicago on July 9th, 2007, into a house built on discipline and quiet sacrifice — my mother a doctor who listens like it's the only thing she has time for, my father an entrepreneur who competes against people who don't believe he can win, and an older brother who set a bar I chased without realizing it. When he skipped a grade, I skipped one too. Then everything moved: a short trip to care for family in India turned permanent, and I learned early that plans change and you adapt anyway.

Family, still the center of the picture
02 — The Court

Love at first serve

2020 · Discovering tennis

In 2020, with the world shut down, my dad handed me a racket and taught me the game himself — and I fell for it instantly, not the winning, but the feeling of reading an opponent and turning a plan into a shot that lands. Down four games to love in one match, I abandoned my serve-and-volley game, remembered my opponent couldn't retrieve a low ball, and rallied my way to a 7-5 win — the day I learned that adapting beats refusing to lose. A growth spurt from 5'10" to 6'6" in three years brought injuries that ended competitive play, but tennis never really left; it just moved to weekend nights with my dad and the neighbors.

Twin Cities Tournament
03 — The Turn

If I am able, then I must

The turnaround years · Valedictorian, 2026

Grades weren't always my story — I struggled straight through 8th grade — until the same discipline I found on the court started bleeding into the classroom, sparked by a single deadweight-loss triangle in 9th-grade economics that I couldn't stop thinking about; that curiosity turned into six patent filings with the US Patent Office, hours tutoring street kids at Nagarajan Academy, fitting prosthetic limbs and raising funding for 45 additional recipients at Bharath Vikas Parishad, and a broken leg in 11th grade that cost five weeks of school but not a single top grade. In 2026, I graduated valedictorian.

Valedictorian · Class of 2026
04 — What's Next

Ready to explore

Carnegie Mellon · Economics & Mathematics

Economics is how I read the world — the choices, the scarcity, the quiet inefficiencies hiding in plain sight — and that's why I'm headed to Carnegie Mellon to study Economics and Math, building the sharpest possible lens for finding what's broken and fixing it in a way that multiplies instead of hoards. I'm walking in at 6'6", carrying a backhand I still bring out on weekends, a stack of patent applications, a habit of reading the news like it owes me an explanation, and a love of anime that teaches me the same thing economics does — every story is a different lens on the same world.

Next piece: Pittsburgh

to be continued...

"Grow through what you go through."

— Maanasa Talluri
